segments.s709-g73: بفرمود is an honorific past verb 'he commanded/said'. تا here is a complementizer 'that/so that' introducing the content of the order. هر که is the relative/indefinite 'whoever/anyone who', مهتر is the noun 'chief, headman', and بدند is a past-form meaning 'they were' (used here to state 'whoever was a chief'); the clause as a whole gives the content of his command.
segments.s709-g74: This clause states the result/location of the order: به فرمان uses به with instrumental force 'by/under (the) command', فرمان is 'command', خراد is used as an epithet 'wise' modifying the following personal name برزین, and شدند is the past/central verb 'they became/were placed' (passive/raising sense). Together: 'they were placed by the command of the wise Barzin.'
segments.s709-g75: The sequence places the verbal-adjective phrase before the copula: به گیتی رونده is a participial/infinitival idea 'to roam/travel the world' (به + گیتی + رونده), بود is the past copula 'was', and کام او 'his desire' is the subject (literally: 'to roam the world was his desire'). رونده functions as a verbal adjective meaning 'one who goes/roams'.
segments.s709-g76: به منشورها is a locative phrase 'on/upon the tablets' (منشورها = inscribed tablets), بر is another locative preposition 'on/upon' used here to emphasize placement, بود is the copula 'was', and نام او 'his name' is the subject: 'his name was upon the tablets.' The word order (locative + بر + بود + subject) is poetic but common in classical Persian.
segments.s709-g77: ز لشکر 'from the army' (ز = from) introduces the group; هر آنکس که is an indefinite relative 'anyone who/whichever person who'; هنگام کار 'at the time of action/engagement' specifies the occasion. This whole segment is a conditional/relativized noun phrase selecting persons from the army.
segments.s709-g78: بماندند is the past plural 'they remained' (describing those people), با is the preposition 'with', نامور is an adjective 'renowned/famous', and شهریار is 'sovereign/king' — together: 'they remained with the renowned sovereign.'
segments.s709-g79: همی here is an emphatic particle ('indeed/just' in this context). خلعت خسروی is the object phrase 'royal robe (of honour)' with خسروی as an adjective 'royal', and دادشان is 'he gave them' (داد + object pronoun). So: 'he gave them the royal robe of honour.'
segments.s709-g80: This clause states what he did next: به شاهی uses به + شاهی to indicate 'to kingship/as rulers' (شاهی = kingship/royal office), به مرزی is the destination 'to the border', and فرستادشان is the past 'he sent them' (with the object pronoun). The doubled به is normal in Persian for marking role ('as') and direction ('to').
segments.s709-g9: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning 'he commanded/said' (subject implied). تا here introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that', so تا + following clause yields 'he commanded that ...'. خلعت is the object 'robe(s) of honour' and آراستند is a past, 3rd-person plural verb 'they adorned/prepared' (آراستن), so the clause literally reads 'he commanded so that they prepared the robe(s) of honour.'
segments.s709-g10: ز در is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'from the gate/from the court' (ز = from, در = gate/door). اسپ is a straightforward noun 'horse/steed' and پرمایگان is the group from whom the things are requested (here 'the great ones / nobles'). خواستند is the past 3rd-person plural of خواستن 'they asked/requested/wanted', so the clause means 'from the gate they requested steeds of the nobles' (i.e. they asked the nobles at the gate for horses).
segments.s709-g11: نیاطوس is the proper name (recipient). را is the definite object marker attaching emphasis/clarity to the recipient. داد is the past tense verb 'gave' (گرفتن/دادن used literally). چندان here means 'so many / to that extent', and گهر is the direct object 'gems/jewels', so the clause reads 'He gave Niyātus so many jewels.'
segments.s709-g12: چه ... و ... is a listing/illustrative construction meaning 'such as / namely'. The sequence lists the particular items given: اسپ 'a horse', پرستار 'an attendant/retainer' (here 'attendant' fits the courtly gift context), and زرین کمر is a compound 'golden belt/girdle' (زرین 'golden' modifies کمر 'girdle/belt'). Grammatically the items follow the demonstrative چه to exemplify the 'چندان گهر' just mentioned.
segments.s709-g13: کز is a relative/causal particle 'that/which / because', introducing the reason clause. اندازه هدیه literally 'the measure/extent of the gift' (اندازه = measure/extent, هدیه = gift). برتر گذاشت is a past construction where برتر 'higher/superior' modifies گذاشت 'he placed/put' — idiomatically 'he set (the gift) above measure' or 'he gave a gift beyond measure' (i.e. a superior/excessive gift). Word order is typical of classical Persian: modifier + verb.
segments.s709-g14: سرش = 'his head' (سر + enclitic possessive ش). را marks سرش as the direct object of برفراشت. ز here is the preposition 'from/with' and پر + مایگان function together as a poetic compound 'the plumes/feathers of the great ones' (پر = plume/feather, مایگان here signals eminent persons). برفراشت is a past, 3rd-person singular causative/active 'he raised/erected/displayed'. The full clause means 'he raised/displayed his head with the plumes of the great (i.e. he put on/displayed a noble plume).'
segments.s709-g15: هر آن is an archaic/poetic temporal or distributive phrase 'every time / whenever / each', followed by شهرکز روم 'the city-centre of Rome' (شهرکز = city-centre, روم = Rome/Byzantium). بستد is the past 3rd-person singular 'he shut/closed', and قباد is the subject 'Qobad'. Note the classical word order: object/noun phrase + verb + subject (here the verb precedes the proper name).
segments.s709-g16: The correlative construction چه ... چه ... means 'whether ... or ...' enumerating examples: هرمز and کسری are two proper names (هرمز = Hormoz, کسری = Kisra/Khosrow). فرخ نژاد is an epithet following کسری: فرخ 'fortunate' modifies نژاد 'born/lineage' to yield 'fortunate-born' (an honorific epithet). Grammatically these words are a loose apposition specifying prominent names.
segments.s709-g33: به آتش is a prepositional phrase (به + noun) meaning 'to/into the fire' placed before the verb for emphasis. بداد here is a poetic past-tense transitive verb 'gave/handed over' (archaic usage) taking the following clause as its object. آنچ پذیرفته بود is a relative-like clause 'that which had been accepted': پذیرفته is a past participle and بود is an auxiliary forming a past-perfect sense ('had been accepted').
segments.s709-g34: سخن هرچ functions as the direct object 'whatever speech/words'; هرچ is an indefinite 'whatever'. پیش ردان is a locative/benefactive phrase 'before/in the presence of the ردان (the rejecters/expellers)', with پیش = 'in the presence of'. گفته بود is a compound past perfect: گفته (past participle 'said') + بود (auxiliary) = 'had said'.
segments.s709-g35: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' placed before a descriptive phrase. زرین و سیمین are coordinate adjectives 'golden and silvery' that precede the following noun. گوهرنگار is a compound meaning 'jeweled' or 'gem‑adorned' describing the material or ornamentation. The whole phrase lists materials or ornamentation introduced by ز.
segments.s709-g36: Again ز introduces a parallel item 'from/due to'; دینار names coinage/money as the material. وز here is an archaic fused form equivalent to 'و ز' ('and from') joining the two parallels; poetically the conjunction and preposition are compacted. گوهر شاهوار is a noun phrase 'regal/lofty gem' where شاهوار modifies گوهر.
segments.s709-g37: به درویش is an indirect-object phrase 'to/for the dervish' placed before the verb for emphasis. بخشید is the simple past transitive verb 'gave/bestowed'. گنج درم are the direct objects ('treasure' and 'dirham' / money); Persian poetry often lists objects in sequence without a linking particle.
segments.s709-g38: نماند is a past negative verb 'did not remain / there remained not'. اندران بوم is a locative phrase 'in that land/among them' (اندران = 'in/among that'); و links a second coordinated phrase. برکس دژم is a noun + adjective 'an obscure/foreign one (برکس) sullen (دژم)', with the adjective following the noun in poetic word order. Altogether: 'Nothing remained in that land, nor any obscure one who was sullen.'
segments.s709-g39: وزان is a compound prepositional/adverbial 'and from that / from that time' introducing a result. جایگه شد is 'became a place' (جایگه = place/position; شد = past 'became'). باندیو شهر is an appositive/name phrase 'the city Bandiu' (city name following or paired with شهر). The sequence is a poetic inversion but follows Persian patterns of placing modifiers before or after the head for rhythm.
segments.s709-g40: که is a subordinating conjunction 'that / so that'. بردارد is a present/subjunctive-form verb here meaning 'may take / may raise / set aside' (purpose or result). از روز شادیش is a prepositional phrase 'from the day of its rejoicing' (شادیش = 'its joy/rejoicing'). بهر is a final preposition 'for / for the sake of'. The clause expresses purpose/result: 'so that from that day it would take for its rejoicing.'
segments.s709-g41: This is a locative clause: کجا begins the clause as an interrogative/relativelike 'where' (here: 'where ... stood'). کشور شورستان is a noun phrase (country + proper-name), with the copula بود in the past (بود = 'was'). مرز follows as a nominative/topic 'its border' — classical Persian often presents location + predicate in this compact order.
segments.s709-g42: This clause states an incapacity/negative scope: کسی is used in the sense 'no one' and governs the verb راندانست ('drove away' / 'could drive away' in past). خاک او = 'his/its soil' (او referring back to the country). ارز ('value; for any price') is placed after the verb-group in an older, idiomatic ordering to mean 'for any price' or 'at any worth'; the sentence implies that nobody, at any price, could drive away even the soil. The modal sense ('could') is implied classically rather than expressed by a separate auxiliary.
segments.s709-g43: به introduces the goal 'to/into' (here 'to the iwan'). که + past perfect کرده بود is a relative clause 'which he had made'; نوشین (delightful) and روان (flowing, i.e. graceful/flowing in appearance or movement) form a descriptive compound modifying ایوان. کرده بود is the pluperfect ('had made/made and was so').
segments.s709-g44: بسی روزگار is an idiomatic classical collocation meaning 'many a time' / 'a long while'. اندر آن = 'in that (place)'. برده بود combines the past participle برده with بود as an auxiliary to yield a pluperfect-like sense 'had spent/ had passed (time)'. The whole segment says he had spent much time there.
segments.s709-g45: گرانمایه (precious/costly) modifies کاخی (a palace); the adjective precedes the noun as is normal. بیاراستند is past-tense plural 'they adorned/embellished' (بی- prefix here part of the verb). The clause is a straightforward SVO: (they) adorned a precious palace.
segments.s709-g46: همان تخت زرین is the direct object phrase 'that golden throne' (همان = 'that', زرین = 'golden'). The verb پیراستند (past plural 'they adorned') follows; the particle به before the verb is an older/poetic placement often used to link object and verb and can have an emphatic or connective function ('and they adorned'). Word order here is object + predicate, typical in epic style.
segments.s709-g47: بیامد is past 'he came'. به تخت پدر is the goal 'to the father's throne'. برنشست is a single past verb meaning 'he sat upon (it)'; the two past verbs in sequence describe consecutive actions by the same subject (implicit 'he'). The structure is compact: subject is understood from context, verbs are both past.
segments.s709-g48: This is a string of epithets/appositive titles describing the subject: جهاندار (sovereign/ruler), پیروز (victorious) and یزدان‌پرست (God-worshipper/pious). Classical Persian often lists such titles without conjunctions; each word is an attributive label for the same referent.
segments.s709-g49: بفرمود is an honorific past verb 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'in order that'). پیش او is a locative phrase 'before him / in his presence' and شد is the past/coercive sense 'became/was made / came to be' (here forming the predicate 'become/serve as scribe'). دبیر is the predicate noun 'scribe/secretary' completing the clause.
segments.s709-g50: This is a noun phrase in apposition specifying who became the scribe: همان 'that same / the very', راهبر 'guide / leader', موبد 'mōbed (Zoroastrian priest)' and تیزویر is the personal/epithet name; adjectives/titles follow in the expected Persian sequence.
segments.s709-g51: نوشتند is the simple past 3rd‑plural 'they wrote/inscribed' with منشور ایرانیان as its direct object: 'the charter (manšūr) of the Iranians.' Word order (verb — object) is common in epic Persian.
segments.s709-g52: برسم here introduces the recipients/result — 'to/for (it) reached' or 'was delivered to' — followed by بزرگان 'the great ones / nobles' and فرخ مهان which is a titled name (Farrokh‑mehan). The clause therefore indicates to whom the written charter was presented.
segments.s709-g53: بدان کار is the prepositional phrase 'for that work' (بدان = به + آن, 'to/for that'), where کار is 'task/work'. بندوی بد کدخدای is a noun phrase: بندوی 'bondman/servant' followed by بد + کدخدای which functions as a name or epithet with کدخدای 'village‑head / local chief' as a title. The whole phrase names the person assigned to the work.
segments.s709-g54: A sequence of adjectival epithets modifying the named kadhkhoda: جهاندیده 'worldly‑wise', و 'and' as a simple coordination, راد 'generous', و 'and', فرخنده‑رای 'auspicious / well‑disposed'. Adjectives follow the noun in this epic register.
segments.s709-g55: This is a transfer clause: خراسان سراسر 'the whole Khorasan' (سراسر = throughout / entire) is the thing given, به گستهم 'to Gostaham' marks the recipient with the preposition به, and داد is the past verb 'he gave' (داد = gave). Word order omits an object marker but the meaning is direct: 'he gave all Khorasan to Gostaham.'
segments.s709-g56: بفرمود تا introduces a purposive/subjunctive clause 'he commanded that...'. نو کند is a present‑subjunctive construction (نو 'new' + کند 'make/do' in subjunctive) meaning 'should make new / renew'. رسم وداد is the object 'the custom/rite of Vidad' (وداد here acts as a proper name), so the full clause means 'he ordered that the custom of Vidad be renewed.'
segments.s709-g65: This phrase groups an adverb, a noun and its owner plus a directional phrase. دگر (more/another) modifies مهر (the gift/favor), خسرو is the possessor placed after the noun in classical style (i.e. “the king’s gift”), and سوی + اندیان is a prepositional phrase meaning “toward the Indians.” The phrase as a whole means “another/gift of Khosrow destined for the Indians.”
segments.s709-g66: بفرمود is an honorific past-tense reportive/command verb “he commanded/said”; it takes the infinitive بردن (“to take/send”) as its complement. برسم here functions like ‘to/into the presence (or court) of’ (from the root رسید/رس) andکیان names the Kayan kings/royal house who are the destination. The whole clause: “he ordered (it) to be taken to the Kayan court.”
segments.s709-g67: Word order here places the recipient before the verb and marks the direct object with را. دگر modifies کشوری to mean “another province/region,” را is the object marker, بگردوی is the (personal) recipient’s name/title, and داد is the past transitive ‘gave.’ The clause reads “(he) gave another province to Begarduy.”
segments.s709-g68: بران means “on that” or “thereupon” referring back to the just‑given province. نامه‌بر is a compound noun ‘letter‑carrier’ or envoy; مهر زرین is a noun + adjective ‘golden seal,’ with مهر here meaning ‘seal’ rather than ‘affection’; and نهاد is the past of نهادن ‘he placed/affixed.’ The clause says he put an envoy bearing a golden seal on/for that (i.e. he dispatched an envoy with a golden seal regarding it).
segments.s709-g69: This segment contains a recipient or locative name followed by the verb and a temporal phrase before the object. ببالوی functions as a personal name/title (recipient), داد is the past tense ‘gave,’ آن زمان is the temporal adverbial ‘at that time,’ and شهر چاچ is the direct object ‘the city of Chach.’ Word order is standard for narrative Persian: recipient/indirect object – verb – time – direct object.
segments.s709-g70: فرستاد is the past transitive ‘he sent’; منشور is the direct object ‘charter/inscribed tablet’; با introduces an accompanying item and تخت عاج is the accompaniment ‘an ivory throne’ (تخت = throne, عاج = ivory). The phrase means he sent a charter along with an ivory throne.
segments.s709-g71: کلید is the noun ‘key(s),’ followed by در گنجها which here functions as a genitive/locative ‘of/in the treasuries’ (در + گنجها). The verbal element بر شمرد (بر + شمرد) means ‘he counted/numbered’ — بر here forms a prefixed verbal sense (‘to enumerate’). Together: ‘he numbered the keys of the treasuries.’
segments.s709-g72: سراسر is an adverb ‘entirely/throughout,’ followed by the recipient name بپور تخواره (here a compound proper name Bupur/Takhvāreh); سپرد is the past transitive ‘entrusted/handed over.’ The sequence means he entrusted all (the treasures/authority) to Bupur Takhvareh.
segments.s709-g57: This phrase expresses the appointment: بهرکار is a prepositional/ purposive phrase (بهر + کار) meaning 'for the work / for the office' and appears before the action as a circumstance. دستور is the noun 'order/command' that names what was given. بد here functions as an archaic/poetic marker attached to the noun phrase to form 'issued an order' (a contracted/old phrase; the normal modern verb 'داد' is not written). برزمهر is a proper name (Barzmehr), the recipient of the order, and thus the logical object of دستور.
segments.s709-g58: This continues and specifies the office given: دبیری is the office/position 'secretaryship' (used as the role Barzmehr receives). جهاندیده is an adjective 'worldly‑wise / experienced' describing the person holding the office; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; خوب‌چهر is another adjective 'fair‑faced / handsome' modifying the same noun. Adjectives follow the head in classical word order here and jointly modify the implied appointee.
segments.s709-g59: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بر کام او is a prepositional phrase 'on his desire / in his favour' (بر + کام + pronoun او). گشت is the past/preterite of گشتن 'turned / became' describing the change of fortune. گردنده چرخ is a descriptive noun phrase 'the revolving wheel' (گردنده participial adjective 'turning/revolving' before the head چرخ 'wheel' used metaphorically for fate); word order places the participle before the noun it modifies.
segments.s709-g60: ببخشید is the past of بخشیدن meaning 'he bestowed/gave' (here 'bestowed' rather than 'forgave'). داراب is the name 'Darab', the indirect object/beneficiary. گرد و صطرخ are two coordinated items given (joined by و); in context they function as names/titles/objects given to Darab. The verb governs the whole coordinated object group.
segments.s709-g61: به is the preposition 'on/onto' (here 'on the charter'). منشور is the direct object 'charter/inscribed document'. برمهر is an adjectival compound 'with a seal / seal‑affixed' (here 'sealed'); زرین is the adjective 'golden'. نهاد is the past of نهادن 'he placed/put' and functions as the main verb taking the preceding phrase as its object ('he placed [a golden seal] on the charter').
segments.s709-g62: یکی is 'one' (a single copy or one such item). درکف is a locative phrase 'in the hand / into the custody of' (در + کف). رام is an epithet 'tame / gentle' modifying the following proper name; برزین is the personal name 'Barzin'. نهاد is again the past 'placed' (he put one into that person's hand). Together this means 'he put one into the hand of the gentle Barzin.'
segments.s709-g63: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past of گفتن/فرمودن meaning 'he ordered / commanded'. تا introduces purpose/result here ('so that / that'), forming a clause of instruction. نزد شاپور is a prepositional phrase 'to the presence of Shapur / before Shapur'. برد is the verb 'to take / bring' (classical past/infinitive form of بردن used after تا to state the ordered action).
segments.s709-g64: پرستنده is a noun 'attendant / worshipper / devotee' — here 'attendant' fits the context. و is 'and' linking two objects. خلعت is 'cloak/robe of honour' (the gift). او را is the object phrase 'his (robe)' with را the definite object marker and او indicating the possessor; سپرد is the past of سپردن 'he handed over / entrusted'. Grammatically the verb سپرد governs the coordinated objects 'پرستنده و خلعت او' (he handed over the attendant and his robe).
segments.s709-g25: پیاده (preverbal adverb) modifies the verb and means 'on foot.' همی‌رفت is the literary past-continuous/iterative form (همی + رفت) — 'he was going' or 'he kept going.' The clause 'و دیده پر آب' is a coordinated verbless description: دیده (eye/eyes) + predicate phrase پر آب (lit. 'full of water') — i.e. 'his eyes were full of (tears).' In Persian the stative predicate (پر + noun) follows the noun it describes.
segments.s709-g26: به introduces a state or circumstance here ('with' or 'in the state of') and links the following nominal phrase to the previous clause. زردی is the abstract noun 'yellowness/sallowness' modifying رخساره ('cheek/complexion'); دو رخساره = 'the two cheeks' (both sides). چون + noun is a simile marker following the thing compared: 'چون آفتاب' = 'like the sun.' Word order (noun + abstract noun/adjective) is typical in poetic Persian.
segments.s709-g27: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' از دربه is a poetic locative phrase referring to the gate/door/entrance (دربه) and is linked by از; نزدیک آتش modifies رسید, so رسید ('arrived/reached') means 'he reached/approached near the fire's door.' Word order places the prepositional phrase before the verb, as is common in older Persian.
segments.s709-g28: شد + adjective/verb = 'became' or 'it happened that.' Here شد is the perfective/past auxiliary meaning 'became' and governs the following phrase. از can mark source/cause ('from' / 'because of'), and آب‌دیده is an adjectival compound 'water-seen' = 'wet with tears/tearful.' رخش is the possessed noun 'his countenance/face' (رخ + possessive marker understood in context). ناپدید is the predicate adjective 'vanished/invisible.' The whole phrase thus reads: 'his tear-wet countenance became invisible.'
segments.s709-g29: دو هفته is a temporal adverbial 'for two weeks' placed before the verb. همی‌خواند is the literary iterative past form 'he kept singing/reciting' (همی + خواند). استا is an archaic/stylistic verb/interjection meaning 'stopped/held' or an exhortation 'stand/stop' depending on context (here it functions as an old-form verb between clauses). وزند (from وزیدن) is a verbal form meaning 'they/it blew' (classical third-person form) — together the small clause describes wind action while the singing continues.
segments.s709-g30: همی‌گشت is another iterative past form 'he kept going / he went about.' بر گرد is a prepositional phrase 'around/about.' آذر نژند is a noun phrase identifying the locus circled; آذر is 'fire' (here referring to the sacred fire) and نژند is an epithet meaning 'base/low' or an attached epithet — together used poetically to name or describe that fire. The verb governs the following locative phrase: 'he kept circling about the (said) fire.'
segments.s709-g31: بهشتم is the possessive noun 'my paradise' (بهشت + ـم). بیامد is the past tense 'came' (literary form). ز + noun is the classical preposition 'from' (ز پـوِکتِک) and آتشکده is the compound noun 'fire-temple' (the sacred place of fire). The phrase literally reads 'my paradise came from the fire-temple' — a poetic way of saying the speaker's joy/vision issued from the fire-temple.
segments.s709-g32: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' نزدیک شد is the two-part predicate 'became near / drew near' (نزدیک + شد). روزگار is 'time/day/season' and سده here denotes the Sadeh festival (a fire-related observance); together روزگار سده = 'the time/day of Sadeh.' The clause therefore means 'when the day/time of Sadeh drew near.'
segments.s709-g17: نیاطوس را داد و بنوشت عهد is two coordinated past actions. نیاطوس + را marks the proper name as the definite direct object; داد is a 3rd-person past 'gave'. The conjunction و links the next past verb بنوشت (classical/poetic past of نوشت ‘wrote’), whose object is عهد 'an oath/pledge'. Word order is verb(s) before the following object (poetic inversion) but each verb governs its object immediately after.
segments.s709-g18: بران جام حنظل پراگند شهد: براَن (بر + آن) = 'on/over that' introduces the prepositional phrase 'on that cup of حنظل (colocynth, i.e. a bitter cup)'. پراگند is an archaic/poetic past of پراکندن 'to scatter/spread' (3rd pl.), and شهد 'honey' is the thing scattered; note the verb precedes its object here, a permissible poetic order.
segments.s709-g19: برفتند پس رومیان سوی روم begins with the past verb برفتند 'they departed/went' (3rd pl.), followed by the temporal/sequence adverb پس 'then'. رومیان is the (understood) subject 'the Romans', and سوی روم is the directional phrase 'toward Rome'. The verb‑first order is a common poetic inversion.
segments.s709-g20: بدان مرز آباد و آباد بوم: بدان is the fused prepositional/demonstrative sense 'to/onto that' (بِ + آن) introducing the destination; مرز آباد = 'prosperous/built-up border/frontier', and و آباد بوم repeats the praise in parallel 'and a prosperous homeland/abode' for poetic emphasis.
segments.s709-g21: دگر هفته برداشت با ده سوار is a temporal + action phrase: دگر هفته 'another week / a week later' sets the time, برداشت here (poetic/past) means 'took up / set out' (took action), and با ده سوار 'with ten horsemen' is a comitative phrase where the numeral precedes the noun (standard Persian order).
segments.s709-g22: که بودند بینا دل و نامدار is a relative clause modifying the horsemen: که 'who/that', بودند past plural 'they were', بینا دل is an adjective phrase 'with clear/valiant heart' (بینا modifying دل), and و نامدار adds 'and famed/renowned'—typical poetic epithets linked by و.
segments.s709-g23: ز لشکر گه آمد به آذرگشسپ: ز (poetic from = از) + لشکر (army/host) with گه (a locative/occasional element, here part of لشکرگاه 'camp/host') yields 'from the camp/host'. آمد is 3rd‑person past 'came', and the proper name آذرگشسپ is the person who arrives (the verb precedes the subject by poetic inversion).
segments.s709-g24: به گنبد نگه کرد و بگذاشت اسپ: به گنبد 'toward/at the dome' is a prepositional phrase; نگه کرد is a light‑verb construction (noun نگه 'gaze' + کردن) meaning 'he looked/gazed', and و بگذاشت اسپ 'and he set down/left the steed' uses بگذاشت (poetic past of گذاشت) with اسپ 'horse' as its object placed after the verb.
segments.s709-g81: همی‌گشت is an imperfective/habitual past-form used in epic style meaning 'kept going' or 'was going about' (verb comes first). گویا here is a particle meaning 'as if / it seems' that modifies the following nominal description. منادیگری is an -گری nominalization 'heralding' or 'being a herald' used as a predicative noun in apposition to the subject.
segments.s709-g82: This is a nominal phrase describing the (apparent) herald: خوش‑آواز 'melodious' (adjective) and بیدار دل 'wakeful/alert‑hearted' (compound adjective where دل 'heart' follows the adjective بیدار). مهتری is the head noun 'chief/elder' in apposition; adjectives precede the noun as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s709-g83: که‌ای here functions as an archaic vocative-like particle addressing people ('O ...'); it precedes زیردستان 'subordinates/subjects'. شاه جهان is a genitival epithet 'king of the world' modifying which king is meant. Word order: vocative + addressee + identifying epithet.
segments.s709-g84: مخوانید is a 2nd‑person plural imperative/subjunctive used here as a command 'sing/recite' (the form is present‑stem imperative in classical Persian). جز 'except' marks the sole thing permitted, آفرین is the exclamation/acclamation 'Afarin' (praise), and در نهان 'in secret' is a prepositional phrase (در + نهان 'secretly') modifying the manner of singing.
segments.s709-g85: مجویید and مریزید are prohibitive forms with the classical negative/prohibitive prefix م‑ (here functioning like 'do not ...'), each addressing the plural addressees. کین 'vengeance' is the object of مجویید, خون 'blood' the object of مریزید; the two verbs are joined by و 'and' to give two linked prohibitions.
segments.s709-g86: مباشید is another prohibitive form 'do not be' (2pl). The noun رهنمون 'guide' is the predicate noun placed at the end, while بر کار بد is a prepositional phrase modifying رهنمون meaning 'to/toward bad deed(s)' — together: 'do not be a guide to evil deeds.' Note the predicate‑final order typical in Persian.
segments.s709-g87: گر is the conditional 'if'. از زیردستان is a prepositional phrase 'because of/from the subordinates' (indicating the source of complaint). بنالد is a classical subjunctive/optative form (b‑ prefix) meaning 'should complain / laments' and کسی 'someone' is the subject — together: 'If someone should complain because of his subordinates...'.
segments.s709-g88: Another conditional clause beginning with گر 'if'. از لشکری 'from an army' marks the agent/source. رنج یابد is a common collocation 'finds suffering / experiences hardship' (یابد = 'finds/obtains' here used as 3rd‑person present/subjunctive). بسی at the end is an adverbial intensifier 'much/plenty' qualifying the suffering.
segments.s709-g97: به پیمان is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning “by/with the pledge” (instrumental sense). که introduces a subordinate clause ‘that…’ and خواند is a past/perfective verb here meaning ‘he uttered/recited.’ بران is an adverbial/locative particle ‘thereupon/on him,’ and آفرین is the noun ‘acclamation/praise’ which is what was uttered—together: ‘by pledge he uttered that acclamation thereupon.’
segments.s709-g98: The initial که here is a relative ’who/that’. کوشد is a present-tense (or habitual/present-subjunctive flavor in epic style) verb ‘strives/endeavors.’ The second که is purposive/complimentary (‘to/that’ introducing the result or goal), followed by آباد دارد زمین, where آباد (adjective) and داشتن (دارد) form a unit meaning ‘to keep/make prosperous’; دارد is third-person singular present and زمین is the direct object ‘the earth/land.’
segments.s709-g99: گر is the conditional particle ‘if.’ ایدون is an archaic/poetic word meaning roughly ‘now/at present’ or ‘appears’ (here functioning to set the temporal frame). The following که is the relative ‘that/who,’ زین سان is an archaic demonstrative phrase ‘in this manner/like this’ (زین = this, سان = like), بود is the past-tense copula ‘was/were,’ and پادشا is ‘king.’ Together they form the protasis: ‘If now there were a king such as this…’
segments.s709-g100: به از is the comparative construction ‘better than’ (به + از = ‘more/better than’). دانشومند is ‘a man of learning’ or ‘scholar,’ and ناپارسا is an adjective ‘impious/ungodly,’ describing that learned man. The whole phrase gives the comparison: ‘better than a learned but impious man.’
segments.s709-g1: This is a compact, somewhat inverted clause typical of classical Persian: the addressee's name (بخراد برزین) comes before the verb of saying/command (بفرمود) and the speaker (شاه) follows as a nominative/title. بفرمود is an honorific past-tense verb 'he said/commanded' used here for the king's utterance. The whole segment functions as 'the king commanded Bakhraad Barzin.'
segments.s709-g2: This is a reported command introduced by که 'that'. رو عرض گه ساز is an instruction: عرضگه (عرض + گه) = 'place of presentation / audience-chamber', with ساز (here an imperative) meaning 'make/construct/prepare' (classical imperative use). و دیوان بخواه links with ساز by coordinating commands: و + دیوان بخواه = 'and summon/call the divan (royal council)'; بخواه is an imperative 'ask/call/summon'.
segments.s709-g3: همه لشکر رومیان is a noun phrase 'the whole/entire army of the Romans/Byzantines' (همه + لشکر + رومیان). عرض کن is an imperative clause 'present/announce/report' with کن the usual colloquial/classical imperative 'do'. The verb applies to the preceding noun phrase as its object.
segments.s709-g4: This is a relative/clausal specification: هر آنکس که هستند = 'every one who is (there/available)'. هر ... آنکس ... که introduces 'every person who ...'. هستند is the present plural copula 'they are / there are'. نوگر کهن is a short coordinate adjective phrase 'new or old' (نوگر = novel/new, کهن = old), qualifying 'هر آنکس'.
segments.s709-g5: درمشان بده is an imperative clause 'give them their dirhams' (درمشان = 'their dirhams/pay' with enclitic -شان 'their', and بده imperative 'give'). The following sequence رومیان را زگنج places 'the Romans' as the object/topic with را and زگنج 'from the treasure', indicating source: '(do not) give to the Romans from the treasury.' Word order is somewhat flexible: the first clause orders immediate payment (درمشان بده), then the speaker qualifies who should not receive treasury funds.
segments.s709-g6: بدادن نباید is an impersonal negative construction 'it must not be given' / 'one should not give'. The following که introduces the purpose/cause (here negative-lest): که بینند رنج = 'so that they do not see / lest they see suffering/humiliation'. بینند is the present-subjunctive/indicative plural 'they see'; رنج = 'suffering / hardship / disgrace'.
segments.s709-g7: کسی کو به خلعت سزاوار بود is a classical relative phrase: کسی 'someone' + کو used here in an old/poetic sense as a relative marker ('who') followed by به خلعت سزاوار بود 'was deserving of the robe (of honour)'. بود is the past copula 'was'. The whole segment identifies the person eligible for the robe.
segments.s709-g8: This is a rhetorical question/remark contrasting deserving men with absence from battle: کجا روز جنگ 'on what day (of battle)' + از در کار بود 'was he away from the field/work' — از marks separation/absence, در کار = 'on the field / in the affair (of battle)'. بود is again past 'was'. The clause implies: there was no day in battle when such a man was absent.
segments.s709-g89: نیابد is a negative present/past-form verb meaning 'does not find' (classical/poetic usage). Here the verb comes before its logical subject (ستمگاره), an inverted order common in poetry: 'does not find the oppressor (a place) except ...'. جز is the exclusive 'except'. دار here is the noun 'gallows/place of execution', and جای is the noun 'place' reinforcing the meaning.
segments.s709-g90: همان رنج و آتش is a noun phrase 'the same suffering and fire'. بدیگر is a fused older form meaning بهِ دیگر ('in/at another'), so بدیگر + سرای = 'in another abode/house/realm'. Note سرای here is the noun 'sara/abode' (poetic spelling/order).
segments.s709-g91: همه (everyone) is the subject and پادشاهند is the predicate 'are kings' (third-person plural). برگنج is بر + گنج 'over/on the treasure' and خویش is the possessive idea 'one's own' built from خوی + enclitic ش (his/their). The phrase says people are 'kings' with respect to their own treasure.
segments.s709-g92: کسی را که is a relative/conditional phrase ('one who' / 'to the one who'); را here is the object/definite marker fused before که. گرد آمد is a past verbal phrase meaning 'has come around' / 'has emerged' (i.e., been freed/returned) and is governed by آمد (past 'came'). از رنج خویش is the complement 'from his suffering' (از = from, رنج = suffering, خویش = his own).
segments.s709-g93: خورید و دهید are imperatives (or jussive forms) meaning 'eat and give' (plural/polite). آنک is an archaic relative/demonstrative 'that which/what', and دارید is present 'you have' (دارید = you possess). چیز means 'thing/matter' — together: 'Eat and give that thing which you have.'
segments.s709-g94: همان is the demonstrative 'the same'. کز = که + از ('that/from which' or 'which is') introduces the relative clause; شماهست is an archaic fused form roughly 'is yours' (literally شُما هست in this lexicon), and درویش نیز names the recipient 'the dervish/poor man, too'. The giving verb earlier is understood to apply to this recipient: 'let the same [thing] which is yours also go to the dervish.'
segments.s709-g95: چو here is the temporal 'when'. باید is the modal 'must/ought' or 'is due' so چو باید ... = 'when it is time/when he ought to'. خورش is 'his food/dish' (the meal), and بامداد پگاه are near-synonymous adverbials 'at dawn/early morning' emphasizing the time of the meal.
segments.s709-g96: سه من is a numeric + unit 'three man' (man is an old weight/measure here). می‌بیابد is the present-habitual/narrative 'he finds/obtains'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', and گنجور شاه is 'the king's treasury/treasure-house' (ز + گنجور = from the treasury of the king).

sentences.s709-s37: He commanded that whoever was a chief should be placed under Kharad Barzin's command.
sentences.s709-s38: His desire was to roam the world; his name stood upon the inscribed tablets.
sentences.s709-s39: From the army, whoever remained at the time of action stayed with the renowned sovereign.
sentences.s709-s40: He bestowed upon them the royal robe of honor and sent them to the border as rulers.
sentences.s709-s5: He commanded that the robes of honour be prepared, and from the gate they requested steeds and nobles.
sentences.s709-s6: He gave Niyātus so many jewels — for example a horse, an attendant, and a golden girdle.
sentences.s709-s7: Because the gift exceeded measure, he set a superior present and raised his head with the plumes of the great.
sentences.s709-s8: Whenever Qobad shut the (Roman) city's centre, whether (it was) Hormoz or Kisra the fortunate-born.
sentences.s709-s17: He threw into the fire that which had been accepted; whatever words he had said before to the rejecters, he had already said.
sentences.s709-s18: From golden and silver (things) jewel‑adorned, from dinars and from gems majestic.
sentences.s709-s19: He bestowed treasure and money upon the dervish; nothing remained in that land, nor any obscure one sullen.
sentences.s709-s20: And from that place it became the city Bandiu, so that from that day it might take its day of rejoicing.
sentences.s709-s21: Where the realm of Shurastan stood — its border, no one could drive away (take) even its soil for any price.
sentences.s709-s22: To the iwan which he had made pleasant and flowing, he had spent many a day there.
sentences.s709-s23: They adorned a costly palace, and they likewise outfitted that golden throne.
sentences.s709-s24: He came to his father's throne and sat upon it — the sovereign, victorious, God-worshipper.
sentences.s709-s25: He ordered that the scribe come before him — the same guide, the mobed Tizvir.
sentences.s709-s26: They wrote the charter of the Iranians and it was delivered to the great men and to Farrokh‑mehan.
sentences.s709-s27: For that task he (appointed) the servant/bondman Bad‑Kadhkhodā — worldly‑wise, generous and well‑disposed.
sentences.s709-s28: He gave the whole of Khorasan to Gostaham; he ordered that Vidad’s custom be made new.
sentences.s709-s33: Again, Khosrow's gift bound for the Indians he commanded to be taken to the court of the Kayan kings.
sentences.s709-s34: He gave another province to Begarduy; on that he set an envoy bearing a golden seal.
sentences.s709-s35: At that time he gave the city of Chach to Babaluy and sent a charter together with an ivory throne.
sentences.s709-s36: He counted (numbered) the keys of the treasuries, then entrusted everything to Bupur Takhvareh.
sentences.s709-s29: He appointed Barzmehr to the office of secretary — worldly‑wise and fair‑faced.
sentences.s709-s30: When the revolving Wheel turned to his desire, he bestowed Gard and Satrakh on Darab.
sentences.s709-s31: He set a golden seal on the charter, and put one (copy) into the hand of the gentle Barzin.
sentences.s709-s32: He ordered that (he) be brought before Shapur, and handed over his attendant and his robe.
sentences.s709-s13: He walked on foot, his eyes full of water, his two cheeks yellow like the sun.
sentences.s709-s14: When he came near the door of the fire, his tear-wet countenance became invisible.
sentences.s709-s15: For two weeks he kept singing; the winds blew, and he kept circling about the fire.
sentences.s709-s16: My paradise came forth from the fire-temple when the time of Sadeh drew near.
sentences.s709-s9: He gave (it) to Niyātus and wrote a pledge; over that cup of colocynth they scattered honey.
sentences.s709-s10: Then the Romans departed toward Rome, to that prosperous frontier and flourishing homeland.
sentences.s709-s11: A week later he set out with ten horsemen, who were stout‑hearted and renowned.
sentences.s709-s12: From the host there came Azar‑Gashasp; he looked toward the dome and set down his horse.
sentences.s709-s41: He kept going, as if heralding — a melodious, wakeful‑hearted chief.
sentences.s709-s42: O subjects of the king of the world, sing nothing except 'Afarin' in secret.
sentences.s709-s43: Do not seek vengeance, and do not shed blood; be not a guide to evil deeds.
sentences.s709-s44: If someone complains about (his) subordinates, or if from an army he finds much suffering,
sentences.s709-s49: By the pledge he uttered that acclamation upon the one who strives to make the land prosper.
sentences.s709-s50: If now there were a king such as this, he would be better than a learned but impious man.
sentences.s709-s1: The king ordered Bakhraad Barzin, "Make an audience-chamber and call the divan."
sentences.s709-s2: Present the whole Roman host; every person who is there, whether new or old.
sentences.s709-s3: Give them their dirhams; do not give the Romans anything from the treasure, lest they see hardship.
sentences.s709-s4: Whoever was deserving of a robe of honour — on what day of battle was he ever out of the field of action?
sentences.s709-s45: The oppressor finds no place except the gallows; the same suffering and fire in another abode.
sentences.s709-s46: Everyone is a king over his own treasure — that belongs only to one who has emerged from his suffering.
sentences.s709-s47: Eat and give away that thing which you have; let that same thing which is yours also go to the poor (dervish).
sentences.s709-s48: When it is time for him to take his morning meal at dawn, the king finds three man (a unit) in the treasury.
